preamble.summary: The dream of Nebuchadnezzar's statue. Theodotion follows the MT closely. The OG has some expansions, particularly in the dialogue scenes, but the core content — the four-kingdom statue and the stone not cut by human hands — is consistent across all versions.
preamble.notable_variants: The OG expands some of Daniel's prayer language and adds minor details to the court scenes. The description of the statue and its interpretation are substantively identical.
preamble.structural_notes: All three versions have 49 verses. The OG occasionally has longer verses with additional clauses.

verses.lxx_reading: ἀπὸ τοῦ αἰῶνος καὶ ἕως τοῦ αἰῶνος
verses.variant_rendering: from the age and unto the age
variant_notes: Standard Greek rendering of the Aramaic idiom. Both mean 'forever and ever.'

verses.variant_rendering: He himself changes seasons and times
variant_notes: Greek reverses the order of 'times and seasons' compared to the Aramaic. OG adds αὐτός (he himself) for emphasis.

verses.lxx_reading: ἀναστήσει ὁ θεὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ βασιλείαν
verses.variant_rendering: the God of heaven will raise up a kingdom
verses.mt_rendering: the God of heaven will set up a kingdom
variant_notes: OG uses anastēsei (raise up), Theodotion uses stēsei (set up). The OG verb is stronger, connoting resurrection language.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
OG adds that Nebuchadnezzar commanded that offerings and incense be brought to Daniel — an expansion not in the MT.

verses.lxx_reading: ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν αὐτός ἐστιν θεὸς τῶν θεῶν
verses.variant_rendering: your God, he is God of gods
verses.mt_rendering: your God is a God of gods
variant_notes: OG emphasizes with autos estin (he is/he himself is), a stronger identification.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
